verses.text: Can Job draw out leviathan with a hook or tame him with a cord? Will the monster plead for mercy or speak soft words? He will do no such thing. The creature man cannot subdue rebukes his pride before God.

Can Job draw out leviathan with a hook or tame him with a cord? Will the monster plead for mercy or speak soft words? He will do no such thing. The creature man cannot subdue rebukes his pride before God.

Can Job draw out leviathan with a hook or tame him with a cord? Will the monster plead for mercy or speak soft words? He will do no such thing. The creature man cannot subdue rebukes his pride before God.
Will leviathan make a covenant to be Job's servant forever, or become merchandise in the market? He is no man's pet or property. What we cannot so much as handle, we surely cannot command.

Will leviathan make a covenant to be Job's servant forever, or become merchandise in the market? He is no man's pet or property. What we cannot so much as handle, we surely cannot command.

None is fierce enough to rouse him; and God asks, 'who then is able to stand before me?' for everything under heaven is His. The argument turns upward. If the beast is untouchable, how much more its Maker.

God describes leviathan's mighty frame and asks who can open the doors of his face, ringed with terrible teeth. His very mouth defies attack. The least part of God's creature overmatches the strength of man.

His rows of scales are shut up tightly together, sealed so close no air comes between. He is armoured beyond breaching. God clothes His creature in defences man could never devise.

His snorting flashes light, his eyes like the dawn, smoke and flame seeming to pour from him, his breath kindling coals. He is terrible to behold. The dread of the creature points to the greater dread of the Creator.

Strength dwells in his neck, and terror goes before him; the mighty are afraid and humble themselves when he raises himself. Even the brave flee him. What then is man before the God who made him?

Sword, spear, dart, and arrow cannot pierce him; he counts iron as straw and brass as rotten wood. No weapon prevails. The force that overpowers all our arms is itself a servant of God.

He churns the deep like a boiling pot, and on earth there is none like him, made without fear, 'a king over all the children of pride.' If no man dares face leviathan, none may defy his Maker. God's greatness, glimpsed in His creatures, lays every proud heart in the dust.
